name: Tunguska Event
description: The Tunguska Event was a massive explosion that occurred near the Podkamennaya Tunguska River in what is now Krasnoyarsk Krai, Russia, on June 30, 1908. The explosion flattened an estimated 80 million trees over an area of 2,150 square kilometers. While the cause remains uncertain, the leading scientific theory is that it was caused by the air burst of a stony meteoroid about 50-60 meters in diameter. Alternative theories abound, ranging from a comet impact or disintegration to a mini black hole passing through the Earth, an antimatter explosion, or even a crashed alien spacecraft. Proponents of these alternative theories often point to the lack of an impact crater, unusual radiation levels (though these are disputed and often attributed to later nuclear testing), and eyewitness accounts describing unusual phenomena before and after the explosion. Mainstream science generally dismisses these alternative explanations due to a lack of concrete evidence and the consistency of the meteoroid airburst theory with observed data and simulations. The event has become a focal point for speculation and pseudo-scientific theories, particularly in Russia, due to the remote location and the lack of immediate, comprehensive investigation after the event. The lasting mystery surrounding the exact nature of the event continues to fuel alternative explanations.
subtitle: Siberian explosion of unexplained origin

claims.claim: The Tunguska Event was caused by an alien spacecraft crash.
claims.evidence: Lack of impact crater, eyewitness accounts of unusual lights and maneuvers before the explosion, purported metallic fragments found in the area.
claims.criticism: No credible evidence of alien technology has been found. The 'metallic fragments' are likely terrestrial materials. Eyewitness accounts are often unreliable and subject to misinterpretation.

claims.claim: The event was triggered by a mini black hole passing through the Earth.
claims.evidence: The lack of a traditional impact crater and the sheer force of the explosion.
claims.criticism: The physics of mini black holes interacting with Earth are poorly understood, and there is no evidence to support the existence of a black hole passing through the area. This theory violates known physical laws.

claims.claim: The Tunguska Event was the result of an antimatter explosion.
claims.evidence: The complete annihilation of matter and the lack of residue.
claims.criticism: Antimatter is rare and unstable. The quantity of antimatter required for such an explosion is astronomically improbable. There is no evidence of the gamma-ray signature associated with antimatter annihilation.

keyFigures.name: Leonid Kulik
keyFigures.role: First major scientific investigator of the site.

keyFigures.name: A.V. Zolotov
keyFigures.role: Proponent of the cometary impact theory.

mythologyConnections: Some link the event to ancient Siberian shamanic beliefs about powerful spirits or celestial beings.
Connections to apocalyptic prophecies in some esoteric circles.

timeline.event: Leonid Kulik's first expedition to the site.
Numerous expeditions and scientific studies conducted.
Continued research and speculation on the cause.

sources.author: Vasilyev, N.V.
sources.work: Early Post-Impact Local Effects at the Fall Site of the Tunguska Meteorite
sources.title: Vasilyev, N.V.
